# The Tears of Kalani

Long ago, when the world was young, the ocean was made of fresh, sweet water. The sea goddess Kalani dwelt in the depths, her heart filled with joy as she watched creatures play in her crystalline realm.

One day, Kalani fell deeply in love with Solari, the sun god who crossed the sky each day. Every dawn, she would rise to the surface to glimpse his radiant face. Every dusk, she would weep as he disappeared beyond the horizon, her tears falling back into the sea.

Solari noticed the beautiful goddess and loved her in return, but their natures were opposed. Whenever he drew near, his heat would cause her waters to steam and vanish. Whenever she reached up to touch him, her cool essence would dim his flames.

They were destined to love from afar.

Heartbroken, Kalani retreated to the ocean floor and wept for forty days and forty nights. Her tears of sorrow were bitter with longing, and they mixed with the waters of the sea. From that day forward, the ocean became salty.

Even now, when the sun sets over the water, the waves rise up toward the sky—Kalani still reaching for her beloved Solari, her salty tears forever preserved in every drop of the sea.

# The First Star

Long ago, when the world was young and darkness covered the sky each night, the people huddled around their fires in fear. The Sun would leave them each evening, and they wondered if it would ever return.

A brave young weaver named Aria refused to accept this fate. She gathered the finest silver threads from spider webs glistening with morning dew and the golden strands of wheat that caught the sunset's last rays. Night after night, she wove these precious materials together, creating a luminous tapestry.

When her work was complete, Aria climbed the highest mountain and called out to the Sky spirits. "We need light in the darkness," she pleaded. "Take my creation and scatter it across your realm."

The Sky spirits, moved by her devotion, took the shimmering tapestry and tore it into countless pieces. Each fragment became a star, hung carefully across the heavens. The spirits granted Aria one final gift: they transformed her into the Moon, so she could watch over her starry children forever.

Since that night, stars have guided travelers through darkness, reminding humanity that courage and creativity can illuminate even the deepest night.

Myth: After Aria became the Moon, she watched over the stars she had created, but soon noticed humanity still struggled below. They moved aimlessly through darkness, unable to find their way home. Though her stars provided light, they scattered randomly across the sky like seeds on wind.

Aria remembered her weaving skills and began to work again. Each night when she was full and bright, she used invisible silver threads—the same material from her original tapestry—to connect certain stars into patterns. She wove the Hunter, so people would remember to gather food. She stitched the Great Bear, teaching them strength and protection. She crafted the Ship, showing them how to sail.

This is why constellations differ across the world: we all weave our own meaning into Aria's stars.
